architecture.summary: ReGuLaR formulates latent reasoning within a Variational Auto-Encoder (VAE) framework, where an LLM samples latent reasoning states from a posterior distribution conditioned on previous states and a question. During training, explicit reasoning chains are rendered as images; a frozen visual encoder (DeepSeek-OCR) extracts dense visual-semantic representations from these rendered images, which regularize the posterior distribution via a learned adapter. The LLM's latent reasoning head generates mean and variance for each latent state; the language head decodes final answers from the latent sequence. Only the latent reasoning head, adapter, and LoRA modules are trained; the backbone LLM and visual encoder remain frozen.
trained_components: latent reasoning head (outputs mean and log-variance of posterior)
adapter (MLP mapping visual representations to latent reasoning space)
LoRA modules on frozen LLM backbone

biblio.title: ReGuLaR: Variational Latent Reasoning Guided by Rendered Chain-of-Thought

claims.statement: ReGuLaR achieves 45.6% accuracy on GSM8K-Aug while reducing average reasoning length to 3.03 steps, compared to CoLaR's 41.7% accuracy at 4.70 steps.
ReGuLaR reduces average reasoning length by approximately 35% compared to CoLaR (from 4.70 to 3.03) while delivering substantial accuracy gains across all datasets.
On GSM-Hard with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B, ReGuLaR achieves higher accuracy than CoLaR (which requires 12.8 reasoning steps) with only 3.1 steps.
In extreme compression (single latent reasoning state) on MATH, ReGuLaR improves average accuracy from 7.76% to 11.9% while reducing reasoning length from 62.2 to 1.00 steps.

On molecular captioning with LLaMA-1B, ReGuLaR achieves state-of-the-art performance across BLEU-2, BLEU-4, METEOR, and ROUGE metrics with a single latent reasoning step, outperforming explicit CoT which uses hundreds of reasoning steps.
ReGuLaR demonstrates consistent positive scaling behavior across model scales (1B to 8B), maintaining significant performance margins over CoLaR and Coconut across all model sizes and datasets.
Compared to token grouping strategies in CoLaR, ReGuLaR's visual-text rendering preserves semantic integrity and provides better regularization with less information loss.

ReGuLaR natively supports multi-modal reasoning by rendering and encoding non-textual elements (charts, graphs, diagrams) alongside text within latent reasoning processes, maintaining standard textual input-output interface.
